prompt: A family has two children. You are told at least one of them is a boy. What is the probability (in %) that both are boys? Assume each child is independently a boy or girl with probability 1/2.
source: Classic conditional-probability problem; appears in Mlodinow, The Drunkard's Walk (2008), ch. 6, and in Mosteller, Fifty Challenging Problems in Probability (1965).
revealExplanation: Before any information, the four equally likely orderings are BB, BG, GB, GG. The condition ‘at least one boy’ rules out GG, leaving three equally likely outcomes: BB, BG, GB. Only one of the three is BB, so the probability is 1/3, not 1/2.
revealWorked: P(BB | at least one boy) = P(BB and at least one boy) / P(at least one boy) = (1/4) / (3/4) = 1/3 ≈ 33.3%.
